questions.question: The primary purpose of dental radiographs is to:
options: Detect conditions not visible clinically such as interproximal caries, bone loss, and periapical pathology
questions.explanation: Radiographs reveal hidden pathology between teeth, within bone, and around root apices.

questions.question: Selection criteria for prescribing dental radiographs are based on:
options: A fixed schedule (e.g., every 6 months for all patients)
Individual patient needs based on clinical findings, risk assessment, and history
questions.explanation: ADA/FDA guidelines recommend radiographs based on clinical judgment, not routine time-based schedules.

questions.question: Bitewing radiographs are primarily indicated for detecting:
options: Interproximal caries and alveolar bone crest levels
questions.explanation: Bitewings show crowns and crestal bone of both arches, ideal for caries and early bone loss detection.

questions.question: Periapical radiographs are indicated when the clinician needs to evaluate:
options: The entire tooth including root apex and surrounding periapical bone
questions.explanation: Periapicals show the complete tooth (crown to apex) plus 2–3 mm of surrounding bone.

questions.question: A full-mouth series (FMX) for an adult typically consists of:
options: 14–20 periapical and bitewing images covering all teeth and surrounding structures
questions.explanation: A standard adult FMX includes periapicals of all tooth-bearing areas plus 4 posterior bitewings.

questions.question: A panoramic radiograph is most useful for evaluating:
options: A broad overview of both jaws, impactions, fractures, pathology, TMJ, and developmental assessment
questions.explanation: Panoramic images provide wide coverage but lack the detail for precise caries detection.

questions.question: The paralleling technique positions the film/sensor:
options: Parallel to the long axis of the tooth, moved toward the midline using a film holder
At an angle bisecting the tooth and film
questions.explanation: Parallelism between tooth and receptor, combined with a perpendicular beam, minimizes distortion.

questions.question: In the paralleling technique, the central ray is directed:
options: Perpendicular to both the long axis of the tooth and the film/sensor
questions.explanation: Perpendicular beam + parallel film/tooth = accurate image without elongation or foreshortening.

questions.question: The paralleling technique requires a longer PID (16 inches) to compensate for:
options: The increased object-to-film distance, which would otherwise cause magnification and blurring
questions.explanation: A longer source-to-film distance produces a more parallel beam, reducing magnification from the increased object-film gap.

questions.question: The paralleling technique is preferred over the bisecting technique because it produces:
options: More dimensionally accurate images with less elongation and foreshortening
questions.explanation: Parallelism between tooth and receptor with a perpendicular beam is geometrically the most accurate configuration.

questions.question: A beam alignment device (XCP ring/arm) is used in the paralleling technique to:
options: Ensure proper PID alignment with the film/sensor, reducing cone cuts and technique errors
questions.explanation: The ring guides the PID to the correct position and angulation for each projection.

questions.question: In the bisecting angle technique, the central ray is directed perpendicular to:
options: The long axis of the tooth
An imaginary line bisecting the angle between the long axis of the tooth and the film
questions.explanation: The bisecting technique directs the beam at the bisector to approximate correct tooth length on the image.

questions.question: The bisecting technique is more prone to image distortion than the paralleling technique because:
options: The film is not parallel to the tooth, and small errors in angulation cause elongation or foreshortening
questions.explanation: Without parallelism, precise angle estimation is critical — and small errors produce noticeable distortion.

questions.question: In the bisecting technique, the patient may hold the film using:
options: Their finger or a disposable film holder (though finger-holding is discouraged)
questions.explanation: While finger-holding is possible, it exposes the patient's finger to radiation and is not recommended.

questions.question: Elongation of the image occurs when the vertical angle is:
questions.explanation: Insufficient vertical angulation stretches the image, making teeth appear longer than they are.

questions.question: Foreshortening of the image occurs when the vertical angle is:
questions.explanation: Excessive vertical angulation compresses the image, making teeth appear shorter.

questions.question: Overlapping of interproximal contacts on a radiograph is caused by:
options: Incorrect horizontal angulation — the beam was not directed through the contact areas
questions.explanation: The beam must be aimed through the contacts perpendicular to the proximal surfaces.

questions.question: A cone cut (partial image) results when:
options: The exposure time is too short
The PID is not properly aligned with the film/sensor, so the beam misses part of the receptor
questions.explanation: The clear (unexposed) area on the film corresponds to the area the beam did not reach.

questions.question: Film reversal (placing the film backwards) produces:
options: A light image with a herringbone (tire track) pattern from the lead foil
questions.explanation: The lead foil absorbs some radiation, lightening the image, and its embossed pattern becomes visible.

questions.question: Patient movement during exposure causes:
questions.explanation: Any motion during the exposure blurs the image because the structures shift relative to the beam and receptor.

questions.question: An overexposed radiograph appears:
questions.explanation: Too many X-ray photons reached the receptor, producing excessive darkness.

questions.question: An underexposed radiograph appears:
questions.explanation: Too few photons reached the receptor, producing an image that is too light for diagnosis.

questions.question: Image density (darkness) is primarily controlled by:
questions.explanation: mAs determines the quantity of X-rays produced, directly controlling how dark the image is.

questions.question: Image contrast is primarily controlled by:
options: kVp — higher kVp produces lower contrast (more gray shades); lower kVp produces higher contrast
questions.explanation: kVp determines beam penetrating power, affecting the range of densities captured (contrast scale).

questions.question: High contrast (short-scale) means the image has:
options: Mostly black and white with few intermediate gray tones
questions.explanation: Short-scale contrast is useful for caries detection but may lose detail in soft tissue areas.

questions.question: Low contrast (long-scale) means the image has:
options: Many shades of gray, showing more subtle density differences
questions.explanation: Long-scale contrast reveals subtle differences in tissue density.

questions.question: Sharpness (resolution) of a radiographic image is improved by:
options: Using a large focal spot and short PID
Using a small focal spot, long PID, minimal object-to-receptor distance, and preventing motion
questions.explanation: Small focal spot, long source-to-object distance, and minimal receptor-to-object distance reduce geometric unsharpness.

questions.question: Scatter radiation reaching the receptor causes:
options: Fog — a uniform gray that reduces contrast and image quality
questions.explanation: Scatter randomly exposes the receptor, adding gray fog that degrades the diagnostic image.

questions.question: The penumbra (geometric unsharpness) of an image can be reduced by:
options: Using a smaller focal spot and increasing the source-to-object distance
questions.explanation: Smaller focal spot and longer source-to-object distance produce sharper images with less edge blurring.

questions.question: On a radiograph, enamel appears:
options: Very light/white (highly radiopaque) — the densest natural tooth structure
questions.explanation: Enamel is ~96% mineralized, absorbing most X-rays and appearing as the brightest natural tooth structure.

questions.question: Dentin appears on a radiograph as:
options: Less radiopaque than enamel — a lighter gray
questions.explanation: Dentin is less mineralized than enamel and appears as a lighter gray.

questions.question: The pulp chamber and root canals appear on a radiograph as:
options: Dark (radiolucent) because pulp is soft tissue
questions.explanation: Soft tissue transmits most X-rays, appearing as dark areas within the tooth.

questions.question: Metallic restorations (amalgam, gold) appear on radiographs as:
options: Very radiopaque (bright white) because metals are dense
questions.explanation: Metals absorb virtually all X-rays and appear as bright white areas.

questions.question: The periodontal ligament (PDL) space appears as a:
options: Thin dark (radiolucent) line between the root and the lamina dura
Thick dark area at the apex
questions.explanation: The PDL is connective tissue (not mineralized), appearing as a thin radiolucent line surrounding the root.

questions.question: The lamina dura appears as a:
options: Thin white (radiopaque) line of dense cortical bone lining the tooth socket (alveolus)
questions.explanation: The lamina dura is the compact bone forming the socket wall — its continuity is a sign of tooth health.

questions.question: Loss or disruption of the lamina dura may indicate:
options: Periapical pathology, periodontal disease, or metabolic conditions
questions.explanation: Pathologic processes that destroy bone (infection, cysts, tumors) disrupt the lamina dura.

questions.question: A periapical radiolucency at the root tip usually suggests:
options: Infection, granuloma, abscess, or cyst at the apex
questions.explanation: Radiolucency at the apex indicates bone destruction from periapical pathology.

questions.question: Interproximal caries appears on a radiograph as:
options: A white area at the contact
A dark (radiolucent) area at or near the contact point between adjacent teeth
A bright spot on the occlusal surface
questions.explanation: Demineralization reduces density, allowing more X-rays to pass through, creating a dark area.

questions.question: Calculus (tartar) on radiographs appears as:
options: Radiopaque (lighter) projections or spurs on the interproximal root surfaces
questions.explanation: Calcified deposits are radiopaque and appear as irregular white projections along root surfaces.

questions.question: The maxillary sinus appears on posterior maxillary periapicals as:
options: A radiolucent (dark) area above the root apices of premolars and molars
questions.explanation: The air-filled sinus appears dark; it should not be confused with pathology.

questions.question: The incisive (nasopalatine) foramen appears on maxillary anterior radiographs as:
options: A small round or oval radiolucency between or near the apices of the central incisors
questions.explanation: This normal anatomic landmark can be mistaken for pathology if its appearance is not recognized.

questions.question: The mental foramen appears on mandibular premolar radiographs as:
options: A small round radiolucency near the apex of the second premolar
questions.explanation: The mental foramen may mimic periapical pathology if superimposed over a root apex.

questions.question: The mandibular canal (inferior alveolar canal) appears as:
options: A radiolucent band bordered by thin radiopaque lines running through the mandibular body
questions.explanation: The canal carries the inferior alveolar neurovascular bundle; its walls of cortical bone create the radiopaque borders.

questions.question: The genial tubercles appear on a mandibular incisor periapical as:
options: A radiopaque ring or bump at the midline of the mandible below the incisor apices
questions.explanation: Genial tubercles are bony projections on the lingual surface of the mandible near the midline.

questions.question: Alveolar bone loss from periodontal disease appears radiographically as:
options: Decreased bone height, loss of the normal pointed interdental septum, and horizontal or vertical bone defects
questions.explanation: Bone loss creates a visible reduction in crestal bone height on radiographs.

questions.question: Horizontal bone loss on a radiograph shows:
options: Bone loss on one side of the tooth only
An even, generalized reduction in bone height across adjacent teeth
An angular defect along one root
questions.explanation: Horizontal loss shows uniform reduction of the alveolar crest across multiple teeth.

questions.question: Vertical (angular) bone loss shows:
options: An angular defect where bone level drops along one surface of a tooth, creating an uneven pattern
questions.explanation: Vertical defects are localized and follow one root surface, creating an angular pattern.

questions.question: Composite restorations on radiographs may appear:
options: Slightly radiopaque to barely visible, depending on filler content — some may be difficult to distinguish from tooth structure
questions.explanation: Modern composites usually contain radiopaque fillers, but older composites may be nearly radiolucent.

questions.question: In labial mounting, radiographs are oriented as if:
options: Viewing the patient face-to-face — the patient's left is on the viewer's right
questions.explanation: The raised (convex) side of the embossed dot faces the viewer; the patient's right is on the viewer's left.

questions.question: The embossed dot on dental film is used for:
options: Film orientation and right/left identification — the raised side faces the tube (source) during exposure
questions.explanation: When correctly placed, the dot identifies the tube side and helps determine the patient's right from left.

questions.question: For horizontal bitewings, the central ray is directed:
options: With a slight positive (+5 to +10°) vertical angulation and horizontal angulation through the contacts
questions.explanation: A slight positive angle directs the beam slightly downward; horizontal angulation must pass through the contacts.

questions.question: Vertical bitewings are preferred over horizontal for patients with:
options: Moderate to advanced periodontal disease requiring visualization of more bone and root structure
questions.explanation: The vertical orientation captures more of the root and alveolar bone, useful for periodontal assessment.

questions.question: A posterior bitewing should capture the distal surface of the:
options: Canine or first premolar through the distal of the last molar (premolar BW) or second/third molar (molar BW)
questions.explanation: Proper coverage ensures all interproximal contacts in the posterior region are captured.

questions.question: An occlusal radiograph is taken with the film placed on the:
options: Occlusal surfaces of the teeth, with the patient biting gently to stabilize it
questions.explanation: Occlusal films are larger and placed across the occlusal surfaces to image a wide area.

questions.question: Occlusal radiographs are useful for locating:
options: Impacted teeth, supernumerary teeth, foreign bodies, fractures, and salivary stones
questions.explanation: The large film area shows a broad view of the arch for evaluating pathology, impactions, and fractures.

questions.question: A panoramic radiograph uses the principle of:
options: Rotational tomography — the tube and receptor rotate around the patient's head
questions.explanation: The tube and receptor move in opposite directions around the patient, capturing a curved focal trough.

questions.question: The focal trough in panoramic radiography is:
options: A three-dimensional horseshoe-shaped zone where structures appear sharply focused
questions.explanation: Only structures within the focal trough appear sharp; those outside it appear blurred or magnified.

questions.question: If the patient is positioned too far forward in the panoramic unit:
options: Anterior teeth appear magnified and blurred
Anterior teeth appear narrowed (minified) and blurred
questions.explanation: Structures anterior to the focal trough appear narrow and out of focus.

questions.question: If the patient is positioned too far back in the panoramic unit:
options: Anterior teeth appear widened (magnified) and blurred
questions.explanation: Structures posterior to the focal trough appear magnified and blurry.

questions.question: Proper patient positioning for a panoramic requires the Frankfort plane (tragus to ala of nose) to be:
questions.explanation: Horizontal Frankfort plane positioning prevents the palate from superimposing on maxillary apices.

questions.question: If the patient's chin is tilted too far down during panoramic imaging:
options: A 'smile' curve of the occlusal plane results
The occlusal plane curves downward ('frown'), and the hard palate may superimpose on maxillary root apices
questions.explanation: Chin too low: flat/frown occlusal plane, possible superimposition of palate on roots.

questions.question: If the patient's chin is tilted too far up during panoramic imaging:
options: An exaggerated upward 'smile' curve of the occlusal plane results
questions.explanation: Chin too high: exaggerated smile curve.

questions.question: Before taking a panoramic radiograph, the patient must remove:
options: All metal objects from the head and neck (glasses, earrings, necklaces, hairpins, removable prostheses)
questions.explanation: Metal objects create artifacts (ghost images) that can obscure diagnostic information.

questions.question: Ghost images on a panoramic radiograph are:
options: Artifacts from dense objects on the opposite side projected as blurred, magnified images on the other side
questions.explanation: Ghost images appear contralateral, higher, and larger than the real object.

questions.question: The tongue should be pressed against the palate during panoramic exposure to:
options: Eliminate the dark air space shadow that obscures maxillary root apices
questions.explanation: The tongue fills the oral airspace; without it, a dark radiolucent band obscures the maxillary apical region.

questions.question: The spine appears on a panoramic radiograph as:
options: A radiopaque vertical column in the midline that can obscure anterior teeth if patient is positioned too far back
questions.explanation: If the patient's head is tilted or positioned too far forward, the spine may superimpose on the anterior region.

questions.question: Patient movement during panoramic exposure causes:
options: Blurring, distortion, or stepping artifacts in the image
questions.explanation: Even slight movement during the 15–20 second exposure significantly degrades the image.

questions.question: Panoramic images inherently magnify structures by approximately:
questions.explanation: The geometry of the rotating system causes objects to be magnified, typically 15–30%.

questions.question: A lead apron is NOT typically used during panoramic radiography because:
options: The beam rotates around the patient and a standard apron may interfere with the image
questions.explanation: The rotating beam makes standard apron shielding impractical; it could block part of the image.

questions.question: The two main types of digital intraoral imaging systems are:
options: Direct digital (CCD/CMOS wired sensors) and indirect digital (PSP/phosphor storage plates)
questions.explanation: CCD/CMOS sensors transmit images via cable in real time; PSP plates are scanned by a laser after exposure.

questions.question: A major advantage of digital radiography over conventional film is:
options: Significantly reduced radiation dose (up to 50–80% less than D-speed film)
questions.explanation: Digital sensors are much more sensitive than film, requiring less radiation for a diagnostic image.

questions.question: Digital radiographic images can be enhanced by adjusting:
options: Brightness, contrast, magnification, and edge enhancement without additional radiation
Only the patient position after exposure
questions.explanation: Post-processing tools allow optimization of the image on screen.

questions.question: CCD/CMOS sensors display the image:
options: Almost immediately on the computer monitor (real-time)
questions.explanation: Wired sensors transmit data instantly, providing near-instantaneous image display.

questions.question: PSP (photostimulable phosphor) plates are processed by:
options: A laser scanner that reads the stored energy as a digital image
questions.explanation: The laser stimulates the phosphor to release stored energy as light, which is converted to a digital image.

questions.question: PSP plates must be erased (cleared) before reuse by:
options: Exposing them to bright light for a specified time to clear residual stored energy
questions.explanation: Bright light erases any residual signal from the plate, preparing it for the next exposure.

questions.question: A pixel is:
options: The smallest unit of a digital image — smaller pixels produce higher resolution
questions.explanation: Pixel size determines spatial resolution: more (smaller) pixels per area = higher detail.

questions.question: Spatial resolution in digital radiography is measured in:
questions.explanation: Lp/mm measures the ability to distinguish fine details: higher lp/mm = better resolution.

questions.question: Digital imaging eliminates the need for:
options: A darkroom, processing chemicals, and film storage
questions.explanation: No chemical processing, no darkroom, and electronic storage replaces physical film storage.

questions.question: The developer solution in manual processing:
options: Reduces exposed silver halide crystals to black metallic silver, creating the visible image
questions.explanation: The developer converts the latent image (exposed crystals) into a visible image of metallic silver.

questions.question: The fixer solution:
options: Removes unexposed silver halide crystals (clearing the film) and hardens the emulsion
questions.explanation: The fixer dissolves unreacted silver halide, making the image permanent and the clear areas transparent.

questions.question: The optimal manual developer temperature is approximately:
questions.explanation: 68°F with approximately 5 minutes development time is the standard for manual processing.

questions.question: Overdeveloped films (too long, too warm) appear:
options: Too dark with increased fog and reduced contrast
questions.explanation: Overdevelopment causes excessive density and chemical fog.

questions.question: Underdeveloped films (too short, too cool, depleted chemicals) appear:
options: Too light with insufficient density and contrast
questions.explanation: Underdevelopment fails to fully reduce exposed silver halide.

questions.question: After fixing, the film must be washed in running water for at least:
questions.explanation: Thorough washing removes residual fixer; incomplete washing causes long-term browning and deterioration.

questions.question: A completely clear film after processing indicates that the film was:
options: Never exposed to X-rays, or placed in fixer before developer
questions.explanation: No exposure means no latent image; the fixer removes all silver halide, leaving clear film.

questions.question: A completely black film after processing indicates:
options: The film was exposed to white light (accidentally opened/light leak) or was severely overexposed
questions.explanation: White light exposes all silver halide crystals, which then all develop to black metallic silver.

questions.question: Brown or yellow staining on processed films is caused by:
options: Insufficient fixing time and/or inadequate washing, leaving residual chemicals
questions.explanation: Residual fixer chemicals oxidize over time, causing discoloration.

questions.question: Reticulation (cracking of the emulsion) is caused by:
options: Extreme temperature differences between processing solutions
questions.explanation: Sudden temperature changes cause the gelatin emulsion to expand/contract rapidly, cracking the surface.

questions.question: Developer spots on a film appear as:
options: Dark spots from premature chemical activation of those areas
questions.explanation: Developer contacting the film before processing causes localized premature development.

questions.question: Fixer spots appear as:
options: White/clear spots where fixer dissolved silver halide before development could occur
questions.explanation: Fixer removes silver halide in contacted areas, preventing development.

questions.question: The darkroom safelight should use:
options: An appropriate filter (e.g., GBX-2 amber/red) at the correct wattage and distance
questions.explanation: The filter wavelength does not expose dental film; wattage and distance must be per manufacturer specifications.

questions.question: A light leak in the darkroom causes:
options: Fogged film — overall gray that reduces contrast
questions.explanation: Any unwanted light reaching unwrapped film causes fog.

questions.question: The coin test checks for:
options: Safelight adequacy — if a coin's outline appears on the developed film, the safelight is fogging the film
questions.explanation: An unwrapped film is placed under the safelight with a coin on it for ~2 minutes. If the coin outline is visible, the safelight is unsafe.

questions.question: Automatic processors differ from manual processing in that they:
options: Use the same temperature and time as manual
Use higher temperatures and roller transport for faster processing (approximately 4–6 minutes)
questions.explanation: Higher temperatures and mechanical transport accelerate the developing, fixing, washing, and drying steps.

questions.question: Replenisher solutions are added to automatic processors to:
options: Maintain chemical strength as solutions become depleted from use and oxidation
questions.explanation: Without replenishment, solution activity declines, producing inconsistent image quality.

questions.question: Films should be stored in:
options: A cool, dry area away from radiation, heat, humidity, and chemicals, and used before expiration
questions.explanation: Proper storage prevents environmental fog and material degradation.

questions.question: Static electricity artifacts appear on film as:
questions.explanation: Static discharge locally exposes the film, creating dark artifact patterns.

questions.question: Scratched films result from:
options: Rough handling, dirty or misaligned processor rollers, or contact with sharp objects
questions.explanation: Scratches appear as white or dark lines depending on whether they occurred before or after exposure.

questions.question: For maxillary molar periapicals, the vertical angulation is approximately:
questions.explanation: The beam is directed downward at approximately +20–30° for maxillary molars.

questions.question: For mandibular anterior periapicals, the vertical angulation is approximately:
questions.explanation: A slight negative (downward) angle is needed for mandibular anteriors.

questions.question: For maxillary anterior periapicals, the vertical angulation is approximately:
questions.explanation: The steep palatal vault requires a more positive (steep) vertical angle for maxillary anteriors.

questions.question: The horizontal angulation for any periapical should direct the central ray:
options: Through the interproximal contact areas to prevent overlapping
questions.explanation: Passing the beam through the contacts separates interproximal surfaces on the image.

questions.question: Faster film speed (e.g., F-speed vs. D-speed) benefits the patient by:
options: Requiring less radiation exposure for an adequate image
questions.explanation: Faster film requires fewer photons to produce an image, directly reducing patient dose.

questions.question: F-speed film requires approximately ___% less radiation than D-speed film.
questions.explanation: F-speed is approximately 60% faster than D-speed, allowing significant dose reduction.

questions.question: A step-wedge (aluminum penetrameter) test is used to:
options: Monitor processing consistency, film/sensor performance, and X-ray output over time
questions.explanation: Regular step-wedge exposures detect changes in system performance.

questions.question: Quality assurance for dental X-ray equipment should include testing of:
options: kVp accuracy, timer accuracy, mA output, beam alignment, and collimation
questions.explanation: Regular QA ensures consistent, diagnostic-quality images at appropriate doses.

questions.question: Automatic processor maintenance should include:
options: Daily cleaning of rollers, regular replenishment of chemicals, temperature monitoring, and periodic deep cleaning
questions.explanation: Consistent processor maintenance prevents artifacts and ensures reproducible image quality.

questions.question: A film-holding device eliminates the need for the patient to:
options: Hold the film with their finger, which would expose their finger to radiation
questions.explanation: Holders maintain film position without finger exposure and improve reproducibility.

questions.question: The tab on a bitewing film is positioned:
options: On the occlusal surface — the patient bites on it to stabilize the film
questions.explanation: The patient's bite on the tab holds the film parallel to the crowns of both arches.

questions.question: The zygomatic process (malar bone) may superimpose on radiographs of:
options: Maxillary premolar and molar root apices
questions.explanation: The dense zygomatic bone overlies the maxillary posterior apical region and can obscure pathology.

questions.question: The coronoid process of the mandible may appear on:
options: Maxillary molar periapicals or tuberosity films as a triangular radiopacity
questions.explanation: When the mouth is open, the coronoid process moves forward and can superimpose on the maxillary molar region.

questions.question: The external oblique ridge appears on mandibular molar periapicals as:
options: A radiopaque line running diagonally across the body/ramus of the mandible
questions.explanation: The external oblique ridge is a dense cortical bone ridge on the external surface of the mandible.

questions.question: The internal oblique ridge (mylohyoid ridge) appears on mandibular molar periapicals as a:
options: Radiopaque line running diagonally below and lingual to the teeth
questions.explanation: The mylohyoid ridge is on the internal mandibular surface and may cross the molar root apices.

questions.question: The mandibular canal appears as a radiolucent band with radiopaque borders because:
options: The canal is filled with bone
The canal contains neurovascular tissue (radiolucent) bounded by cortical bone walls (radiopaque)
The canal is filled with metal
questions.explanation: The nerve and vessels within the canal are soft tissue (dark); the cortical walls are bone (light).

questions.question: The median palatal suture appears on maxillary occlusal radiographs as:
options: A thin radiolucent line running anteroposterior in the midline of the palate
questions.explanation: The suture between the two palatine processes appears as a fine dark line.

questions.question: The nasal fossa appears on maxillary anterior periapicals as:
options: Bilateral radiolucent areas above the maxillary central and lateral incisors
questions.explanation: The air-filled nasal passages appear dark above the maxillary anterior apices.

questions.question: The nasal septum appears on maxillary anterior periapicals as:
options: A radiopaque vertical line dividing the nasal fossa in the midline
questions.explanation: The bony/cartilaginous septum is denser than the air-filled nasal cavities.

questions.question: The anterior nasal spine appears as a:
options: A V-shaped radiopacity at the base of the nasal septum, at the floor of the nasal cavity
questions.explanation: This bony projection at the anterior junction of the maxillae is visible on maxillary anterior films.

questions.question: The nutrient canals appear on radiographs as:
options: Thin radiolucent vertical lines, usually in the anterior mandible, representing channels for blood vessels
questions.explanation: These small vascular channels may be seen as fine dark lines between mandibular incisors.

questions.question: A radiopaque restoration that appears to extend into the pulp on a radiograph likely indicates:
options: A cavity liner or base beneath the restoration
questions.explanation: The radiopaque liner/base material is often visible as a thin bright line between the restoration and the pulp.

questions.question: When a patient has a strong gag reflex, which technique modification can help?
options: Having the patient breathe through their nose, placing mandibular films first, using a smaller sensor, and applying topical anesthetic on the palate
questions.explanation: Multiple strategies reduce gagging: nasal breathing, distraction, desensitization, and prioritizing less-triggering areas first.

questions.question: For edentulous areas, periapical films should be positioned to capture:
options: Nothing — edentulous areas don't need imaging
The alveolar ridge to evaluate for retained roots, pathology, or implant planning
questions.explanation: Edentulous areas may harbor retained roots, cysts, or other pathology relevant to treatment planning.

questions.question: When imaging a child, exposure factors (mA, kVp, time) should be:
options: The same as for an adult
Reduced compared to adult settings due to smaller anatomy and greater radiosensitivity
Set to maximum for all children
questions.explanation: Smaller patient size requires less radiation; pediatric protocols significantly reduce dose.

questions.question: For a child, a smaller film size (size 0 or 1) is used because:
options: The smaller oral cavity cannot accommodate adult-sized (size 2) films comfortably
Smaller films produce better images always
questions.explanation: Size 0 (periapical) or size 1 are more comfortable and easier to position in a child's mouth.

questions.question: A periapical radiograph should show at least ___ of bone beyond the root apex.
questions.explanation: At least 2–3 mm of periapical bone ensures any pathology at the apex is captured.

questions.question: If the image of a maxillary premolar periapical shows the zygomatic process superimposed over the apices, the correction is to:
options: Increase the vertical angulation slightly to project the zygomatic process above the roots
questions.explanation: A steeper downward angle shifts the zygomatic shadow superiorly, away from the apices.

questions.question: Digital sensors are thicker and more rigid than film, which can cause:
options: Patient discomfort and difficulty with sensor placement, especially in the posterior and on pediatric patients
questions.explanation: The rigid, bulky sensor can be uncomfortable; cushioned edges and gentle technique help.

questions.question: PSP plates have an advantage over CCD/CMOS sensors in that PSP plates are:
options: Wireless, flexible (thinner), and more similar in size/feel to conventional film
questions.explanation: PSP plates are cordless and thinner, making them more comfortable and easier to position.

questions.question: A disadvantage of PSP plates compared to CCD/CMOS sensors is:
options: They require a separate scanning step, making image display slower than direct digital sensors
questions.explanation: PSP plates must be scanned after exposure, adding a processing step before the image is available.

questions.question: The paralleling technique is best accomplished with which PID length?
questions.explanation: A 16-inch PID produces a more parallel beam, compensating for the increased object-to-film distance.

questions.question: The tube shift (buccal object) rule is used to determine the:
options: Buccal-lingual position of an object by taking two radiographs with a horizontal tube shift
questions.explanation: SLOB: Same Lingual, Opposite Buccal — a lingual object moves in the same direction as the tube shift.

questions.question: SLOB stands for:
options: Same Lingual, Opposite Buccal — describing how objects move relative to tube shift direction
questions.explanation: An object on the lingual moves the Same direction as the tube; a buccal object moves the Opposite direction.

questions.question: CBCT (Cone Beam Computed Tomography) provides:
options: 3D volumetric images of dental and maxillofacial structures
questions.explanation: CBCT captures a 3D volume, useful for implant planning, impactions, pathology, and airway evaluation.

questions.question: CBCT delivers a radiation dose that is:
options: Higher than conventional dental radiographs but significantly lower than medical CT
questions.explanation: CBCT dose is much less than medical CT but substantially more than standard dental films.

questions.question: A cephalometric radiograph is primarily used in:
options: Orthodontic treatment planning to analyze skeletal and dental relationships
questions.explanation: Lateral cephalometric films allow measurement of skeletal landmarks for orthodontic diagnosis.

questions.question: A voxel in 3D digital imaging is the equivalent of a ___ in 2D imaging.
questions.explanation: A voxel is a volumetric pixel — the smallest discrete element of a 3D dataset.

questions.question: Radiopaque structures appear ___ on a radiograph because they ___ more X-rays.
questions.explanation: Dense structures stop more photons, resulting in less exposure of the receptor and a lighter appearance.

questions.question: Radiolucent structures appear ___ on a radiograph because they allow ___ X-rays to pass through.
questions.explanation: Less dense structures transmit more photons to the receptor, creating darker areas.

questions.question: Air spaces appear on radiographs as:
options: Very dark (radiolucent) because air has virtually no density to absorb X-rays
questions.explanation: Air offers negligible X-ray attenuation, so maximum photons reach the receptor.

questions.question: Bone appears on radiographs as:
options: Varying shades of light gray to white (radiopaque), depending on density and thickness — cortical bone is brighter than cancellous
questions.explanation: Dense cortical bone appears brighter than porous cancellous (spongy) bone.

questions.question: When evaluating a bitewing for interproximal caries, the assistant looks for:
options: Bright white areas at the contacts
Dark (radiolucent) shadows at or near the contact points between teeth
questions.explanation: Carious demineralization reduces the density of enamel/dentin, creating a dark zone at the contact area.

questions.question: The developing agent hydroquinone primarily produces:
options: High contrast — the blacks in the image (acts slowly)
questions.explanation: Hydroquinone is a slow-acting agent responsible for the blacks (high-density areas) of the image.

questions.question: The developing agent Elon (metol) primarily produces:
options: Gray tones rapidly — responsible for image detail and density in the middle range
questions.explanation: Elon works fast, producing the range of gray tones. Together with hydroquinone, a full tonal range is achieved.

questions.question: The fixing agent in dental fixer is:
questions.explanation: Ammonium thiosulfate ('hypo') dissolves and removes the unexposed silver halide crystals.

questions.question: The hardener in the fixer solution is:
questions.explanation: Potassium alum hardens and shrinks the emulsion gelatin, making it more durable for handling and storage.

questions.question: Subject contrast is determined by:
options: Differences in thickness, density, and atomic number of the structures being radiographed
The type of film holder used
questions.explanation: Tissues of different compositions absorb X-rays differently, creating the contrast visible on the image.

questions.question: Increasing mA while keeping kVp and time constant will:
options: Increase density (the image becomes darker) without changing contrast
questions.explanation: Higher mA produces more X-ray photons, increasing overall image density.

questions.question: Doubling the exposure time while keeping other factors constant will:
options: Double the density (the image becomes darker)
questions.explanation: Exposure time is directly proportional to the number of X-rays and therefore image density.

questions.question: The hamular process (hamulus) of the medial pterygoid plate may appear on:
options: Maxillary molar or tuberosity periapicals as a small hook-shaped radiopacity posterior to the tuberosity
questions.explanation: The hamulus is a thin bony projection posterior to the maxillary tuberosity.

questions.question: The maxillary tuberosity appears on radiographs as:
options: A rounded radiopaque area distal to the last maxillary molar
questions.explanation: The tuberosity is the bony bulge at the posterior end of the maxillary alveolar ridge.

questions.question: Which size film is used for adult periapical radiographs?
questions.explanation: Size 2 (31 × 41 mm) is the standard adult periapical film. Size 0 is for small children; size 4 is for occlusal projections.

questions.question: Which size film is used for occlusal radiographs?
questions.explanation: Size 4 (57 × 76 mm) is the large occlusal film placed on the biting surface.

questions.question: The white (plain) side of the film packet faces:
options: The back of the patient's head
questions.explanation: The tube side (white, plain side with the embossed dot) must face the X-ray source for proper exposure.

questions.question: The lead foil inside the film packet is positioned on the:
options: Back side (away from the source) to absorb exit radiation and reduce backscatter
Lead foil is not present in dental film
questions.explanation: The lead foil reduces backscatter and protects the film from secondary radiation from behind.

questions.question: Double exposure results in:
options: A superimposed (ghost-like double) image that is dark and undiagnostic
questions.explanation: Two separate exposures on the same receptor create overlapping images.

questions.question: Bending the film before exposure causes:
options: A dark curved line (crescent-shaped artifact) across the image from cracking the emulsion
questions.explanation: Bending creases the emulsion, causing localized exposure that appears as a dark crescent.

questions.question: When taking posterior bitewings, the beam alignment should direct the central ray through the interproximal contacts of which teeth?
options: Premolars on one film and molars on another, passing through each set of contacts
questions.explanation: Premolar and molar bitewings each cover different contact areas.

questions.question: The lead apron is typically NOT worn during panoramic imaging because:
options: The rotating beam may project the apron's image onto the film, creating artifacts
Lead aprons are illegal for panoramic use
questions.explanation: The apron could obstruct the beam path during its rotation around the patient.

questions.question: The inverted Y formed by the nasal fossa and maxillary sinus is visible on:
questions.explanation: The radiopaque lines of the nasal fossa floor and sinus wall form an inverted Y near the canine apex.

questions.question: Rectangular collimation reduces patient radiation exposure compared to round collimation by approximately:
questions.explanation: Rectangular PID restricts the beam to the size of the receptor, significantly reducing irradiated tissue volume.

questions.question: The minimum total filtration for dental X-ray machines operating above 70 kVp is:
questions.explanation: Federal regulations require ≥2.5 mm Al total filtration for units above 70 kVp to remove low-energy photons.

questions.question: An advantage of PSP plates over CCD sensors for intraoral imaging is that PSP plates are:
options: Wired to a computer for instant display
Cordless, thin, and flexible — more comfortable for patients
Always faster to display than CCD
questions.explanation: PSP plates handle more like conventional film, improving patient comfort.

questions.question: A panoramic radiograph is NOT the best choice for evaluating:
questions.explanation: The resolution and geometry of panoramic imaging make it suboptimal for detecting small proximal caries.

questions.question: For endodontic working length determination, a periapical radiograph must show:
options: The entire root including the apex with 2–3 mm of periapical bone, with the file in the canal
Only the crown and adjacent tooth
questions.explanation: Working length films must show the file tip relative to the apex for accurate measurement.

questions.question: Quantum noise (mottle) in digital imaging increases when:
options: The dose is very low, resulting in too few photons reaching the sensor
questions.explanation: At very low exposures, statistical variation in the few photons creates a speckled (noisy) image.

questions.question: Increasing kVp from 60 to 70 approximately doubles the beam intensity. To maintain the same density, the operator should:
options: Reduce the mAs by approximately half
questions.explanation: The 15% kVp rule: a 15% increase in kVp doubles intensity, so mAs should be halved.

questions.question: The water bath in manual processing is used to:
options: Rinse the film between developer and fixer, and maintain solution temperature
questions.explanation: The rinse step prevents chemical carryover; the water bath maintains consistent temperatures.

questions.question: Adequate fixation time in manual processing is typically:
options: At least twice the clearing time (usually 2–4 minutes total, but 10 min is often recommended)
questions.explanation: The film should be fixed for at least twice the time it takes to clear to ensure complete removal of unexposed silver halide.

questions.question: When taking a mandibular molar periapical, the film should be placed:
options: Parallel to the long axis of the mandibular molars, positioned lingually toward the floor of the mouth using a holder
questions.explanation: The film is positioned lingual to the teeth to achieve parallelism.

questions.question: The XCP (extension cone paralleling) system includes which components?
options: A bite block (film/sensor holder), an indicator ring, and a connecting arm
questions.explanation: The three parts work together to position the receptor, align the beam, and guide the PID.

questions.question: The lips should be positioned ___ during a panoramic radiograph.
questions.explanation: Lips closed eliminates the air shadow between the lips and teeth that creates a dark artifact across the anterior region.

questions.question: A periapical abscess vs. a normal anatomic foramen can be distinguished by:
options: Location relative to tooth apices, associated symptoms, clinical testing (vitality), and correlation with clinical findings
questions.explanation: Normal foramina are predictably located; pathology corresponds to non-vital teeth and clinical symptoms.

questions.question: On a bitewing radiograph, early enamel caries appears as a:
options: Small radiolucent (dark) notch or shadow confined to the enamel at the contact point
Large dark area extending to the pulp
questions.explanation: Early enamel caries creates a small V-shaped or triangular radiolucency at the proximal contact area.

questions.question: Caries that has penetrated through the DEJ into dentin on a bitewing appears as:
options: A small dot in enamel only
A wider radiolucent area that spreads along the DEJ into dentin, often mushroom-shaped
questions.explanation: Caries spreads along the DEJ because dentin is softer and less mineralized than enamel.

questions.question: Cervical burnout on a radiograph refers to:
options: Actual caries at the neck of the tooth
A radiolucent artifact at the CEJ caused by the thin enamel and dentin in the cervical area, mimicking caries
questions.explanation: The thin cervical area naturally appears darker and can be misdiagnosed as cervical caries.

questions.question: For a posterior periapical of the maxillary premolars, the film should capture:
options: Both premolars and the distal of the canine
questions.explanation: Proper premolar periapicals show both premolars and at least the distal surface of the canine.

questions.question: When using a digital sensor with a beam alignment device, the PID should be aligned to the:
options: Indicator ring/arm of the holder, which positions the beam perpendicular to the sensor
questions.explanation: The ring guides the PID to the correct position for that specific projection.

questions.question: The dynamic range of digital sensors refers to their ability to:
options: Capture a wider range of exposures (from underexposed to overexposed) and still produce a diagnostic image
questions.explanation: Digital sensors have a broader latitude than film, meaning minor exposure errors are more forgiving.

questions.question: Teleradiology refers to:
options: The electronic transmission of digital radiographic images to remote locations for consultation or interpretation
questions.explanation: Teleradiology allows specialists to review images remotely, improving access to expert consultation.

questions.question: DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine) is a:
options: Standard format for storing and transmitting medical/dental digital images
questions.explanation: DICOM ensures that digital images from different manufacturers can be shared and viewed universally.

questions.question: If the automatic processor is producing dark films, possible causes include:
options: Developer temperature too high, over-replenishment, or contaminated solutions
questions.explanation: Excessive developer activity (too hot, too concentrated) produces overdeveloped, dark films.

questions.question: If the automatic processor is producing light films, possible causes include:
options: Developer temperature too low, depleted/exhausted developer, or insufficient development time
Excess light in the room only
questions.explanation: Insufficient developer activity (cold, weak, or expired) produces underdeveloped, light films.

questions.question: The anode heel effect causes:
options: Greater X-ray intensity at the cathode end of the beam than at the anode end
Better resolution at the anode end
questions.explanation: The anode target's angle causes self-absorption at the anode end; the cathode end has higher intensity.

questions.question: To compensate for the anode heel effect, the thicker part of the patient should be positioned toward the:
questions.explanation: Placing thicker structures (molars) at the cathode end and thinner structures (premolars) at the anode end helps equalize density.

questions.question: A radiopaque finding near the apex of a mandibular premolar that is round and well-defined is most likely the:
questions.explanation: The mental foramen is a common normal radiolucent landmark near the premolar apices — but if round and radiopaque, consider a condensing osteitis or other finding. The context here suggests radiolucent: most commonly this is the mental foramen.

questions.question: A panoramic image showing a 'reverse smile' (downward curve) of the occlusal plane indicates:
options: The patient's chin was too far up
The patient's chin was too far down
questions.explanation: Chin too low causes the occlusal plane to flatten or curve downward.

questions.question: An earring left in place during a panoramic will appear as:
options: A real image on one side and a ghost image (magnified and blurred) on the other side
questions.explanation: Dense objects create both a real and a ghost image on opposite sides of the panoramic film.

questions.question: The exposure switch for a dental X-ray machine must be located:
options: In the operatory next to the patient
At least 6 feet from the tubehead, or behind a protective barrier
questions.explanation: The operator must be at a safe distance or behind shielding during exposure.

questions.question: A 'J-shaped' radiolucency around a root tip most likely represents:
options: A periapical granuloma or cyst associated with a non-vital tooth
questions.explanation: A well-defined radiolucency at the apex is a classic radiographic finding for chronic periapical pathology.

questions.question: The purpose of the collimator on a dental X-ray unit is to:
options: Restrict the size and shape of the X-ray beam to reduce the volume of irradiated tissue
questions.explanation: Collimation limits the beam to the area of interest, reducing patient exposure.

questions.question: The purpose of the aluminum filter in the tubehead is to:
options: Remove low-energy (soft) X-rays that would be absorbed by the patient without contributing to the image
questions.explanation: Filtration removes photons too weak to reach the receptor, reducing skin dose.

questions.question: A pointed (conical) PID is no longer recommended because it:
options: Creates scatter radiation at its closed end, increasing patient exposure
questions.explanation: The closed end of a pointed cone generates secondary radiation. Open-ended PIDs eliminate this.

questions.question: When using digital sensors, if the image appears too light, the operator should:
options: Increase the exposure settings slightly (mAs) for the next exposure
questions.explanation: Increasing mAs provides more photons to the sensor, improving image density.

questions.question: Digital image storage requires:
options: Secure electronic storage with backup systems, HIPAA-compliant access controls, and adequate server capacity
questions.explanation: Digital records must be securely stored, backed up, and accessible only to authorized personnel.

questions.question: The infection control barrier on a digital sensor must be checked after each exposure for:
options: Tears or punctures that could allow contamination of the sensor
questions.explanation: A compromised barrier allows saliva and blood to contact the sensor, requiring disinfection per manufacturer guidelines.

questions.question: A torus (e.g., torus palatinus or mandibularis) appears on a radiograph as:
options: A well-defined radiopaque (dense bone) area superimposed over the teeth or alveolar ridge
questions.explanation: Tori are dense bone exostoses that appear as radiopaque areas.

questions.question: An impacted tooth appears on a radiograph as:
options: A tooth that is completely or partially embedded in bone and/or soft tissue, visible in its unerupted position
questions.explanation: The impacted tooth, crown, and surrounding follicular space are all visible radiographically.

questions.question: A dentigerous cyst appears on a radiograph as:
options: A well-defined radiolucent area surrounding the crown of an unerupted tooth
A dark line along the root
questions.explanation: Dentigerous cysts develop around the crown of an unerupted tooth within the dental follicle.

questions.question: Root resorption appears on a radiograph as:
options: Shortening or blunting of the root apex, with a ragged or irregular appearance
questions.explanation: Resorption causes loss of root structure, visible as a shortened or blunted root tip.

questions.question: Pulp stones (denticles) appear on radiographs as:
options: Small round or oval radiopaque areas within the pulp chamber
questions.explanation: Calcified masses within the pulp chamber are visible as small bright dots.

questions.question: When exposing dental radiographs on a pregnant patient, the MOST important protective measure is:
options: Using a lead apron with thyroid collar, minimizing retakes, and only taking radiographs when clinically necessary
Using no protection since dental radiation is minimal
questions.explanation: Radiographs can be taken when needed with proper shielding and ALARA principles.

questions.question: The standard number of posterior bitewings for an adult is:
options: 2 on each side (4 total): premolar and molar bitewings
questions.explanation: Standard adult bitewings include premolar and molar films bilaterally.

questions.question: A periapical radiograph that shows the entire tooth but cuts off the apex is corrected by:
options: Repositioning the film/sensor more apically (toward the floor of mouth for mandibular, toward palate for maxillary)
questions.explanation: The receptor must be positioned to include the apex plus 2-3 mm of periapical bone.

questions.question: The Frankfort plane is the imaginary line from the:
options: Tragus of the ear to the ala (wing) of the nose
Ear to the crown of the head
questions.explanation: The Frankfort plane is used as a positioning reference for panoramic and cephalometric radiography.

questions.question: The dental assistant should NEVER hold the film in the patient's mouth because:
options: It exposes the assistant's fingers to the primary X-ray beam
questions.explanation: Film holders or the patient (if necessary) should stabilize the receptor — never the operator.

questions.question: A widened PDL space around a tooth may indicate:
options: Trauma, occlusal trauma, orthodontic forces, or periapical/periodontal pathology
questions.explanation: Widened PDL space is a sign of pathologic or excessive forces on the tooth.

questions.question: A common artifact on panoramic images caused by the patient's tongue not being raised against the palate is:
options: A bright white band across the maxillary teeth
A dark (radiolucent) band across the maxillary apical region, obscuring root tips
A ghost image of the spine
A doubled image of the mandible
questions.explanation: The air space between the tongue and palate creates a dark shadow over the maxillary apices.

questions.question: For a patient with a shallow palatal vault, film placement for maxillary periapicals can be improved by:
options: Using a cotton roll between the film and the palate to push the film down toward the teeth
questions.explanation: A cotton roll cushion helps seat the film closer to the teeth in patients with flat palates.

questions.question: The submandibular fossa appears on mandibular molar periapicals as:
options: A radiolucent area below the mylohyoid ridge and mandibular molar roots
questions.explanation: The depression on the lingual surface of the mandible housing the submandibular gland appears dark.

questions.question: A sensor that is accidentally exposed to radiation without being connected to the computer will:
options: Produce no image — the exposure data is lost since the sensor was not active/recording
Store the image for later retrieval automatically
questions.explanation: CCD/CMOS sensors must be active and connected during exposure to capture the image.

questions.question: Recurrent (secondary) caries around an existing restoration appears as:
options: A bright white area at the margin
A radiolucent (dark) area at the margin of the restoration, indicating new decay beneath or around it
questions.explanation: New decay at the restoration-tooth interface creates a radiolucent zone at the margins.

questions.question: When taking radiographs on a child, the number of films should be:
options: The same as an adult FMX
Based on the child's age, size, dentition, and clinical indications — typically fewer films than an adult
questions.explanation: Pediatric imaging follows the ALARA principle and is limited to clinically necessary images.

questions.question: An anterior periapical should capture how many teeth?
options: 2–3 teeth including the tooth of interest and adjacent structures
All anterior teeth on one film
questions.explanation: Each anterior periapical covers approximately 2-3 teeth in the field of view.

questions.question: The inverse square law states that radiation intensity is:
options: Inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the source
questions.explanation: Doubling the distance reduces intensity to one-quarter. This applies to both patient dose and operator protection.

questions.question: A long PID (16 inch) produces a more parallel beam than a short PID (8 inch), resulting in:
questions.explanation: The longer source-to-object distance provides a more parallel, uniform beam.

questions.question: For mandibular premolar periapicals, the vertical angulation is approximately:
questions.explanation: A mild negative (downward) angle is used for mandibular premolar projections.

questions.question: For mandibular molar periapicals, the vertical angulation is approximately:
questions.explanation: Mandibular molar periapicals use a slight to moderate negative angulation.

questions.question: If a PSP plate is not scanned within a reasonable time after exposure:
options: The stored energy gradually fades, degrading image quality
questions.explanation: PSP plates lose signal over time; they should be scanned promptly (within minutes to hours).

questions.question: The magnification in panoramic imaging means that measurements taken from panoramic films are:
options: NOT dimensionally accurate — they overestimate actual sizes
questions.explanation: The inherent magnification makes panoramic images unsuitable for precise linear measurements.

questions.question: A radiolucent crescent at the base of a film after processing is most likely caused by:
options: Bending or crimping the film before exposure, which cracks the emulsion
questions.explanation: Film bending creates a stress crescent artifact.

questions.question: The rinse step between developer and fixer in manual processing serves to:
options: Remove excess developer from the film to prevent contamination of the fixer
questions.explanation: Rinsing extends fixer life and prevents chemical cross-contamination.

questions.question: Film fog can result from all EXCEPT:
options: Using the correct exposure settings and proper storage
questions.explanation: Correct techniques and proper storage prevent fog. Expired film, bad safelights, and environmental radiation cause it.

questions.question: An external root resorption appears as:
options: Loss of root structure at the apex or along the lateral root surface, visible as shortened or irregular root contour
questions.explanation: External resorption results from inflammatory or traumatic processes.

questions.question: Internal resorption appears on a radiograph as:
options: A thin dark line around the root
A well-defined, balloon-shaped radiolucent area within the root canal or pulp chamber
A bright white spot in the pulp
questions.explanation: Internal resorption causes enlargement of the canal or chamber, visible as a dark widening.

questions.question: The inferior border of the mandible appears on mandibular molar periapicals as a:
options: Dense radiopaque line at the lower edge of the film
questions.explanation: The cortical bone of the inferior mandibular border is dense and clearly radiopaque.

questions.question: Hypercementosis appears on a radiograph as:
options: Excessive cementum deposition causing bulbous enlargement of the root, which remains surrounded by a normal PDL space and lamina dura
A dark area at the apex
questions.explanation: The enlarged root maintains normal PDL and lamina dura, distinguishing it from pathology.

questions.question: If a patient cannot tolerate intraoral sensors/film due to a severe gag reflex, an alternative imaging option is:
options: A panoramic radiograph as a screening image, supplemented with targeted intraoral views if possible
questions.explanation: Panoramic imaging avoids intraoral placement and may be supplemented with individual films as tolerated.

questions.question: The ideal angulation for a posterior bitewing has the central ray entering slightly _____ relative to the occlusal plane.
questions.explanation: A slight positive vertical angulation allows the beam to pass between the contacts of upper and lower teeth.

questions.question: A periapical cemental dysplasia (periapical cementoma) in its early stage appears as:
options: A periapical radiolucency at the apex of a vital mandibular anterior tooth
A dark line along the root
questions.explanation: Early periapical cemental dysplasia mimics an abscess radiographically but the tooth tests vital — no treatment needed.

questions.question: If a panoramic image shows the mandibular rami very narrow and the anterior teeth magnified, the patient was likely positioned:
options: Too far back (posterior) relative to the focal trough
questions.explanation: Posterior positioning magnifies anterior structures and narrows the rami.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an acceptable method for stabilizing the film in the patient's mouth?
options: The operator's finger holding the film
questions.explanation: The operator must never hold the film — this violates radiation protection principles.

questions.question: Film speed is classified by letters. Which of these is the fastest?
questions.explanation: F-speed is the fastest currently available intraoral film, requiring the least radiation.

questions.question: When digital sensors break or malfunction, the practice should:
options: Discard the sensor and avoid digital imaging
Contact the manufacturer for repair/replacement and use backup sensors or PSP plates in the interim
questions.explanation: Having backup sensors or PSP plates ensures continuity of digital imaging services.

questions.question: Extraoral radiographs include all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Periapical films are intraoral. Panoramic, cephalometric, and skull radiographs are extraoral.

questions.question: A lateral jaw radiograph is an extraoral film used to evaluate:
options: The body and ramus of the mandible, fractures, and large lesions
questions.explanation: Lateral jaw projections provide broad views of the mandible for assessing pathology and fractures.

questions.question: The field of view (FOV) in CBCT refers to:
options: The volume of anatomy captured by the scanner — smaller FOV reduces dose and improves resolution for the area of interest
questions.explanation: Selecting the smallest FOV that answers the clinical question minimizes dose.

questions.question: Image artifacts in digital radiography can be caused by:
options: Damaged sensors, scratched PSP plates, electronic interference, or software errors
Nothing — digital images have no artifacts
questions.explanation: Digital systems have unique artifact sources related to hardware and software.

questions.question: If a panoramic radiograph shows a unilateral magnification of one side of the image, the most likely cause is:
options: The patient's head was rotated (turned) to one side during the exposure
questions.explanation: Head rotation places one side of the face closer to (or farther from) the receptor, causing asymmetric magnification.

questions.question: The dental radiographic image is a ___ dimensional representation of ___ dimensional structures.
questions.explanation: Radiographs compress 3D anatomy into a 2D image, which can cause superimposition of structures.

questions.question: Superimposition of structures on a 2D radiograph can make diagnosis difficult. The best way to resolve ambiguity is:
options: Taking additional views at different angles, using clinical correlation, and/or using CBCT for 3D evaluation
Increasing exposure to make the image darker
questions.explanation: Multiple angles and 3D imaging help separate superimposed structures.

questions.question: A BID (beam-indicating device) is another name for the:
questions.explanation: BID and PID are interchangeable terms for the cone that aims the X-ray beam.

questions.question: The floor of the nasal cavity appears on maxillary periapicals as a:
options: Radiopaque horizontal line above the apices of maxillary incisors
questions.explanation: The dense cortical bone forming the nasal floor creates a distinct radiopaque line.

questions.question: A supernumerary tooth (e.g., mesiodens) appears on a radiograph as:
options: An additional tooth-like structure, typically between the maxillary central incisors for a mesiodens
questions.explanation: Supernumerary teeth are extra teeth visible as additional tooth structures.

questions.question: Dental caries on the occlusal surface of a molar is difficult to detect radiographically because:
options: Caries doesn't occur on occlusal surfaces
The thick buccal and lingual enamel walls superimpose over the occlusal surface, masking early lesions
questions.explanation: Occlusal caries must penetrate significantly into dentin before it becomes visible radiographically due to superimposition.

questions.question: When taking radiographs of a patient with a maxillary torus, the assistant should:
options: Use smaller sensors/film, place them to one side of the torus, and seat them slowly and carefully for patient comfort
Have the patient remove the torus
questions.explanation: Tori can make film placement painful; careful technique and smaller receptors improve comfort.

questions.question: Color enhancement (pseudocolor) in digital imaging:
options: Assigns colors to different density levels to help visualize subtle differences, but does not change the underlying data
questions.explanation: Pseudocolor is a visualization tool that can highlight density differences not easily seen in grayscale.

questions.question: Potassium bromide in the developer solution acts as a:
options: Restrainer — it prevents the developer from acting on unexposed crystals, reducing fog
questions.explanation: Potassium bromide limits development to only exposed crystals, controlling fog.

questions.question: Sodium sulfite in the developer acts as a:
options: Preservative — it prevents rapid oxidation of the developing agents
questions.explanation: Sodium sulfite protects the developing agents from oxidation, extending solution life.

questions.question: The ideal operator position during dental radiographic exposure is:
options: At least 6 feet from the source, at 90–135° to the primary beam, or behind a barrier
Directly in front of the beam
questions.explanation: Distance, angle, and shielding are the three principles of operator radiation protection.

questions.question: A pediatric patient requiring a bitewing radiograph should have:
options: The same film/sensor size as an adult
A smaller receptor (size 0 or 1) and reduced exposure settings
questions.explanation: Smaller receptors fit comfortably; reduced mAs matches smaller tissue thickness.

questions.question: X-rays are produced in the dental X-ray tube when:
options: High-speed electrons strike the tungsten target (anode)
questions.explanation: Electrons from the heated cathode filament are accelerated to the anode target, where their kinetic energy is converted to X-ray photons and heat.

questions.question: The cathode of the X-ray tube contains:
options: The filament that produces electrons via thermionic emission
questions.explanation: When the tungsten filament is heated, it releases electrons (thermionic emission) that form the electron cloud.

questions.question: The anode of the X-ray tube contains:
options: The tungsten target (focal spot) embedded in a copper stem
questions.explanation: The focal spot on the tungsten target is where X-rays are generated. Copper dissipates heat.

questions.question: Approximately what percentage of energy at the anode is converted to X-rays?
questions.explanation: Only ~1% of kinetic energy becomes X-rays; ~99% is wasted as heat.

questions.question: X-rays are a form of:
questions.explanation: X-rays are electromagnetic radiation with short wavelengths and high energy.

questions.question: Properties of X-rays include all EXCEPT:
options: Travel at the speed of light
Can be focused by a magnetic lens like visible light
questions.explanation: X-rays cannot be focused by conventional lenses. They travel in straight lines and cannot be bent by magnets.

questions.question: kVp (kilovoltage peak) controls the:
options: Quality (penetrating power/energy) of the X-ray beam
questions.explanation: Higher kVp = faster electrons = higher-energy photons = more penetrating beam.

questions.question: mA (milliamperage) controls the:
options: Quantity (number) of X-rays produced per unit time
questions.explanation: Higher mA = more electrons flowing = more X-ray photons produced.

questions.question: Bremsstrahlung radiation is produced when:
options: High-speed electrons are decelerated near tungsten nuclei, releasing energy as X-ray photons
questions.explanation: Bremsstrahlung ('braking radiation') produces a continuous spectrum of X-ray energies.

questions.question: Characteristic radiation is produced when:
options: An electron is decelerated by the nucleus
An incoming electron dislodges an inner-shell electron from a tungsten atom, and an outer-shell electron fills the vacancy, releasing a specific-energy photon
questions.explanation: Characteristic radiation has discrete energy levels specific to the tungsten target.

questions.question: The Compton effect involves:
options: An X-ray photon interacting with an outer-shell electron, producing a scattered photon of lower energy and a recoil electron
questions.explanation: Compton scatter is the primary source of scatter radiation in diagnostic radiology.

questions.question: Photoelectric absorption involves:
options: Complete absorption of the X-ray photon by an inner-shell electron, which is ejected from the atom
questions.explanation: Photoelectric absorption is responsible for image contrast — denser tissues absorb more photons.

questions.question: Scatter radiation is produced primarily by:
options: Compton interactions within the patient's tissues
questions.explanation: Patient tissues scatter incoming photons in random directions via Compton interactions.

questions.question: The Roentgen (R) measures:
questions.explanation: The Roentgen quantifies ionization produced by X-rays in air.

questions.question: The Gray (Gy) or rad measures:
questions.explanation: 1 Gy = 100 rad. It measures energy absorbed per unit mass of tissue.

questions.question: The Sievert (Sv) or rem measures:
options: Dose equivalent — accounting for the biological effectiveness of different radiation types
questions.explanation: 1 Sv = 100 rem. The Sievert weights absorbed dose by the type of radiation.

questions.question: 1 Gray equals:
questions.explanation: The Gray is the SI unit; 1 Gy = 100 rad.

questions.question: 1 Sievert equals:
questions.explanation: The Sievert is the SI unit; 1 Sv = 100 rem.

questions.question: The law of Bergonié and Tribondeau states that cells are more radiosensitive when they are:
options: Rapidly dividing, immature (undifferentiated), and have a long mitotic future
questions.explanation: Young, rapidly dividing, undifferentiated cells are most vulnerable to radiation damage.

questions.question: The MOST radiosensitive cells in the body are:
questions.explanation: Lymphocytes are the most radiosensitive somatic cells.

questions.question: The LEAST radiosensitive cells are:
questions.explanation: Mature, non-dividing nerve and muscle cells are the most radioresistant.

questions.question: Radiation damages cells primarily by damaging:
questions.explanation: DNA is the critical target; damage can cause cell death, mutation, or carcinogenesis.

questions.question: Indirect radiation damage occurs through:
options: Free radicals (especially hydroxyl radicals) formed from the radiolysis of water
questions.explanation: ~80% of the body is water; ionization of water produces free radicals that damage DNA.

questions.question: Stochastic effects of radiation:
options: Have NO known threshold; probability increases with dose but severity does not (e.g., cancer, genetic effects)
questions.explanation: The linear non-threshold model assumes any dose carries some risk of stochastic effects.

questions.question: Deterministic (nonstochastic) effects:
options: Have a threshold dose; severity increases with dose above the threshold (e.g., cataracts, skin burns)
questions.explanation: Below the threshold, no effect occurs. Above it, severity increases with increasing dose.

questions.question: Somatic effects affect:
questions.explanation: Somatic effects manifest in the person who was exposed (cancer, cataracts, skin changes).

questions.question: Genetic (hereditary) effects affect:
options: The offspring of the irradiated individual through damaged reproductive cells
questions.explanation: Genetic effects result from radiation damage to eggs or sperm, potentially affecting future generations.

questions.question: The latent period is the time between:
options: Radiation exposure and the appearance of clinical symptoms
questions.explanation: The latent period can be days to decades, depending on the dose and type of effect.

questions.question: The annual occupational dose limit for the whole body is:
questions.explanation: NCRP recommends a maximum of 50 mSv/year for occupationally exposed workers.

questions.question: The annual dose limit for the general public (non-occupational) is:
questions.explanation: The public dose limit is 1/50th of the occupational limit.

questions.question: The dose to the embryo/fetus of a pregnant worker should not exceed:
options: 5 mSv (0.5 rem) for the entire pregnancy
questions.explanation: Additional monthly limits of 0.5 mSv apply.

questions.question: The period of greatest fetal radiosensitivity is:
questions.explanation: Rapid cell division and organogenesis make the first trimester the most vulnerable period.

questions.question: The linear non-threshold (LNT) model assumes:
options: There is a safe threshold dose
Any amount of radiation carries some risk; risk increases linearly with dose
Radiation is harmless at low doses
questions.explanation: LNT is the basis for ALARA — no dose is considered absolutely safe.

questions.question: ALARA stands for:
questions.explanation: ALARA guides radiation protection: achieve the diagnostic objective with the minimum possible dose.

questions.question: The lead apron protects the patient from:
options: Scatter radiation to radiosensitive organs (gonads, thyroid, bone marrow)
questions.explanation: The lead apron attenuates scatter radiation reaching the patient's trunk.

questions.question: A thyroid collar should be used:
options: During intraoral radiographic exposures to protect the thyroid gland
questions.explanation: The thyroid is radiosensitive; a collar shields it from scatter during intraoral exposures.

questions.question: Rectangular collimation reduces patient dose compared to round collimation by:
questions.explanation: A rectangular PID restricts the beam to match the receptor size, reducing the irradiated tissue volume.

questions.question: The aluminum filter removes:
options: Low-energy (soft) photons that would increase patient skin dose without contributing to the image
questions.explanation: Filtration 'hardens' the beam by removing weak photons absorbed in superficial tissue.

questions.question: Using the fastest available film speed or digital sensors benefits the patient by:
options: Requiring less radiation for an adequate image
questions.explanation: Faster receptors require fewer photons, directly reducing patient exposure.

questions.question: Retakes increase patient dose. Retakes are minimized by:
options: Proper training, correct technique, film holders, quality control, and careful patient positioning
Taking fewer images than clinically needed
